terrain_focus: Mix flat roads and hilly trails - include at least one hill session
pace_guidance: Long run: 10-11 min/mile conversational pace. Mid runs: 9-10 min/mile steady.
elevation_note: Include 2000-3000ft elevation gain on your long run this week to simulate race terrain.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: You're 8 weeks out from your 100km. This is your peak phase - your body is at its strongest. This week's target: 63 miles with a 30-mile long run. Trust the process - every mile this week is money in the bank for race day.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 30 miles on the schedule today. Start slow - first 5 miles should feel almost too easy. Mix in some hills if you can. Remember: this is about time on feet, not speed. You've got this.

terrain_focus: Push the hilly trails on your long run - back-to-back hills build mental toughness
pace_guidance: Long run: 10-11 min/mile. Mid runs: 9-10 min/mile. Easy days: whatever feels good.
elevation_note: Aim for 3000+ ft elevation gain on the long run. Practice power hiking uphills - you'll use this on race day.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: 7 weeks to race day. You're in the thick of peak training - this is the hardest part of the cycle and you're showing up. Target: 65 miles this week. Slight bump from last week to keep building. Your consistency is your superpower.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 31 miles today. Treat this like a dress rehearsal - practice your race nutrition, test your gear, and run the terrain you'll face on May 9. The goal isn't speed, it's learning what works for your body over long distances.

terrain_focus: Longest run of the cycle - choose your most race-like terrain
pace_guidance: Long run: 10-11 min/mile. Practice negative splitting - start slower, finish slightly faster.
elevation_note: This is your biggest elevation week. 3000-3500ft on the long run. After this, it gets easier.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: 6 weeks out. This is the peak of your peak - the biggest training week. After this, the hard part is done and we start dialing back. Target: 65 miles with your longest run of the cycle at 32 miles. Embrace the challenge - this is where race fitness is forged.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 32 miles - your longest run before race day. This is a mental milestone as much as a physical one. After today, you'll know you can cover serious distance. Run your own race, eat early and often, and finish strong. This is the run you'll remember on May 9.

terrain_focus: Slightly reduced volume - mix of trails and roads, nothing new or extreme
pace_guidance: Long run: 10-11 min/mile comfortable. Mid runs: maintain 9-10 min/mile. Listen to your legs.
elevation_note: Dial back to 2000-2500ft on the long run. You've already proven you can handle the big climbs.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: 5 weeks out. We're easing off the gas slightly - 60 miles this week with a 28-mile long run. You crushed last week's peak. This small step-back lets your body absorb that fitness. Think of it like this: training tears you down, rest builds you up. Both matter.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 28 miles today - a step back from last week and that's intentional. Your body is consolidating the fitness you built. Run at a comfortable effort, enjoy it, and remember that smart training means knowing when to push and when to cruise.

terrain_focus: Familiar routes only - no experiments. Keep trails moderate.
pace_guidance: Long run: 10-11 min/mile easy. Mid runs: 9:30-10 min/mile. Everything should feel controlled.
elevation_note: Cap elevation at 1500ft on the long run. Save your legs - the hard climbing is behind you.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: 4 weeks to race day - welcome to pre-taper. Mileage drops to 50 this week. This might feel weird after weeks of 60+. That's normal. Research shows that fitness peaks 2-3 weeks AFTER your hardest training, not during it. The work is done - now we let it bloom.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 20 miles today. Noticeably shorter than recent weeks - and your legs might feel surprisingly fresh. That's the fitness adapting. Enjoy the feeling. Run smooth, practice your race-day nutrition one more time, and finish feeling like you could do more. That's exactly the point.

terrain_focus: Mostly flat with one moderate trail run. Keep it comfortable.
pace_guidance: Long run: 10-11 min/mile. Mid runs: easy effort. No speed work.
elevation_note: Keep it under 1000ft total. Flat is fine. Your legs know what hills feel like.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: 3 weeks out. Mileage drops again to 42 miles. You might start feeling antsy or doubting yourself - Am I losing fitness? Am I doing enough? This is completely normal and even has a name: taper anxiety. The science is clear: reducing volume while maintaining easy runs lets your muscles fully repair and your glycogen stores max out. You're getting STRONGER by running less right now.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 18 miles - easy and controlled. This is about keeping the engine warm, not building more fitness. You've already built it. If your brain says go harder, politely ignore it. Finish feeling fresh. Race day is where you empty the tank, not today.

terrain_focus: Easy routes only. Flat roads or gentle trails. Nothing technical.
pace_guidance: Everything at easy conversational pace. If you can't chat comfortably, slow down.
elevation_note: Minimal elevation. Keep it flat. Your legs are storing energy for race day.
sunday_message: Weekly Check-In: 2 weeks to go. Full taper mode - 30 miles this week. That's less than half your peak. You might feel restless, sluggish, or moody. Taper tantrums are real and they're a GOOD sign - it means your body is redirecting energy to recovery. Sleep as much as you can. Eat well. Hydrate. The hay is in the barn.
tuesday_message: Long Run Day: 12 miles - your shortest 'long run' in months. Keep it flat and easy. Use this run to finalize your race day plan: what you'll eat, what you'll wear, when you'll take fuel. Mental preparation is physical preparation. You're almost there.

terrain_focus: Short shakeout runs only. 2-4 miles max. Flat and easy.
pace_guidance: Just move your legs. 10-12 min/mile jogs. This is about blood flow, not training.
elevation_note: Zero elevation seeking. Flat only. Save everything for Saturday.
sunday_message: Race Week Check-In: This is it. 5 days to your 100km. This week is about rest, nutrition, and confidence. Run only short shakeout jogs - 2 to 4 miles max. Carb-load Wednesday through Friday. Lay out your gear by Thursday. You've put in the miles, you've done the climbs, you've built the engine. Now trust it.
tuesday_message: Final Shakeout: Just a quick 3-4 mile jog today. Shake the legs out, nothing more. Spend the rest of your energy visualizing race day - the start, the middle miles, the finish. You've trained for this. Saturday is your day. Go get it.
